name: Hecatoncheires
description: The Hecatoncheires, also known as the Centimanes, are primordial giants in Greek mythology, possessing immense strength and distinguished by their hundred hands and fifty heads. Born from Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky), they were named Briareus (also known as Aigaion), Cottus, and Gyges. Their father, Uranus, feared their power and imprisoned them within the depths of Tartarus, the abyss below the underworld. This act caused Gaia great pain, prompting her to create the Titans to overthrow Uranus. However, the Titans also feared the Hecatoncheires and kept them imprisoned. It was Zeus who eventually freed the Hecatoncheires to aid him in his battle against the Titans, known as the Titanomachy. Their overwhelming strength and ability to hurl hundreds of rocks at once proved instrumental in securing victory for the Olympians, establishing Zeus's reign and a new cosmic order. Following the war, the Hecatoncheires were appointed as guards of Tartarus, ensuring the Titans remained imprisoned.
subtitle: Hundred-Handed Giants of Greek Myth
appearance: Gigantic beings with fifty heads and one hundred arms, possessing immense physical power. Their appearance inspires dread and awe.
habitat: Initially imprisoned in Tartarus, later appointed as guardians of Tartarus.
behavior: Loyal to Zeus after being freed, acting as steadfast and unwavering guardians of Tartarus. Before their release, they were victims of imprisonment and fear.

keyMyths.title: The Birth and Imprisonment of the Hecatoncheires
keyMyths.description: Born of Gaia and Uranus, the Hecatoncheires were immediately imprisoned by their father due to his fear of their immense power. This imprisonment caused Gaia great suffering and set in motion the events leading to the Titanomachy. Their initial imprisonment highlights the fear of primordial forces within the established order.

keyMyths.title: The Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: Zeus freed the Hecatoncheires from Tartarus to aid the Olympians in their war against the Titans. Their hundred hands proved devastating in battle, allowing them to overwhelm the Titans with a barrage of rocks. Their participation was crucial to the Olympian victory, demonstrating the importance of unleashing primordial forces to establish a new order.

keyMyths.title: Guardians of Tartarus
keyMyths.description: After the Titanomachy, Zeus appointed the Hecatoncheires as guardians of Tartarus, where the defeated Titans were imprisoned. This role highlights their immense strength and unwavering loyalty to Zeus, as they were tasked with containing the very forces that threatened to overthrow the Olympian order. They represent the ultimate jailers.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides the foundational account of the Hecatoncheires' birth, imprisonment, and role in the Titanomachy.
sources.text: Provides the foundational account of the Hecatoncheires' birth, imprisonment, and role in the Titanomachy.
